args_redacted.query: zero trust security model principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy explained

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  Zero Trust Security vs. Defense in Depth: A Comparative Analysis
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
  Zero Trust is a security model built on the principle of "never trust, always
  verify." It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the network,
  and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by default.
  Every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously
  validated before granting access to resources.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
  Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy inspired by military
  fortification tactics. It deploys multiple, overlapping layers of security
  controls so that if one layer fails, the next layer continues to provide
  protection. The goal is redundancy and resilience through diversity of
  controls.

================================================================================

2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  a) Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize based on all available
     data points (identity, location, device health, data classification).
  b) Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum access needed, and only
     for the duration required (Just-In-Time / Just-Enough-Access).
  c) Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised.
     Segment access, encrypt everywhere, and use continuous monitoring.
  d) Micro-Segmentation — Divide the network into isolated zones; lateral
     movement is blocked by default.
  e) Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent; it is constantly
     re-evaluated based on behavior and context.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  a) Layered Controls — Deploy physical, technical, and administrative
     controls across multiple tiers (perimeter, network, host, application,
     data).
  b) Redundancy — No single control is sufficient; overlapping protections
     ensure that failure of one layer does not result in total compromise.
  c) Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (firewalls,
     IDS/IPS, antivirus, encryption, access controls, training) so that a
     single attack technique cannot bypass all layers.
  d) Perimeter Focus — Traditional implementations emphasize a strong outer
     perimeter (though modern versions extend beyond this).
  e) Detection and Response — Layers include not just prevention but also
     detection, logging, and incident response capabilities.

================================================================================

3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Aspect               | Zero Trust                     | Defense in Depth              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Core Philosophy      | Trust nothing, verify          | Layer everything, hope one    |
|                      | everything continuously        | layer stops the attacker      |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Network Assumption   | Network is always hostile;     | Internal network is safer     |
|                      | no implicit trust for internal | than external; perimeter      |
|                      | traffic                        | provides a trust boundary     |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Primary Focus        | Identity and data-centric      | Infrastructure and perimeter- |
|                      | security                       | centric security              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Access Control       | Dynamic, context-aware,        | Often static, role-based,     |
|                      | continuously re-evaluated      | session-based                 |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement     | Blocked by micro-segmentation  | May be possible once inside   |
|                      | and default-deny policies      | the perimeter                 |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Trust Model          | No implicit trust anywhere     | Implicit trust inside the     |
|                      |                                | perimeter (in traditional     |
|                      |                                | implementations)              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Architecture         | Identity-aware proxy / SDP     | Firewalls, VLANs, VPNs, DMZs  |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Response to Breach   | Assume breach; limit blast     | Detect breach; contain via    |
|                      | radius via segmentation        | layered controls              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|

================================================================================

4. SIMILARITIES AND COMPLEMENTARY ASPECTS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  • Both aim to reduce risk and limit the impact of a security breach.
  • Both advocate for multiple controls — Zero Trust through continuous
    verification at every access

2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  a) Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize based on all available

  e) Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent; it is constantly
     re-evaluated based on behavior and context.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  a) Layered Controls — Deploy physical, technical, and administrative

  d) Perimeter Focus — Traditional implementations emphasize a strong outer

  e) Detection and Response — Layers include not just prevention but also
     detection, logging, and incident response capabilities.

================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Aspect               | Zero Trust                     | Defense in Depth              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|

|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|

|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Access Control       | Dynamic, context-aware,        | Often static, role-based,     |
|                      | continuously re-evaluated      | session-based                 |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement     | Blocked by micro-segmentation  | May be possible once inside   |
|                      | and default-deny policies      | the perimeter                 |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Trust Model          | No implicit trust anywhere     | Implicit trust inside the     |
|                      |                                | perimeter (in traditional     |
|                      |                                | implementations)              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Architecture         | Identity-aware proxy / SDP     | Firewalls, VLANs, VPNs, DMZs  |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Response to Breach   | Assume breach; limit blast     | Detect breach; contain via    |
|                      | radius via segmentation        | layered controls              |
|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|

================================================================================

4. SIMILARITIES AND COMPLEMENTARY ASPECTS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    overlapping layers of different control types.
